data.title: PGIM Target Date Funds Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
PGIM Target Date Funds Overview

The PGIM Target Date Funds combine PGIM's asset management capabilities, insights into participant behavior, and Prudential's leadership in pension risk transfer. Together, these provide us with a unique vantage point to assess the retirement challenge, understand its complexities, and offer a target date fund solution designed to work under the broadest set of circumstances.

The PGIM Target Date Funds are a suite of target date funds that offers:

  • A differentiated, three-stage glidepath designed to solve for the right risks at the right time, seeking to improve outcomes by mitigating key risks participants face as they prepare for retirement.
  • Exposure to a diversified array of asset classes, including equities, fixed income, and non-traditional asset classes like TIPS, commodities, and real estate.
  • A combination of active and passive investment management strategies designed to reduce costs while adding value in areas with greater alpha opportunities.

A Differentiated Glidepath — Solves for the Right Risks at the Right Time

  1. 1 Accumulation stage: Higher equity exposure when the greatest risk may be not taking enough risk
  2. 2 Preservation stage: Lower equity exposure when participants may have the most to lose and don't have time to recover losses
  3. 3 Inflation Protection stage: Focus on assets that may help preserve purchasing power for 30+ years in retirement

The Fund's allocation is ratcheted annually along the glidepath. The subadviser will review the glidepath and allocations annually, at its discretion.

Note: The Fund is "ratcheted" annually to shift the Fund's allocation gradually from equity investments toward fixed income investments in accordance with the current Glidepath. Additionally, the Fund's subadviser will review the current Glidepath and the Fund's asset allocations to Underlying Funds annually to determine, in its discretion, whether the then current Glidepath allocations remain appropriate to meet the Fund's investment objective. Based on such reviews, the subadviser may, without prior notification to shareholders, make changes to the current Glidepath and/or the Fund's asset allocations as it deems appropriate to meet the Fund's investment objective in light of market and economic conditions and such other factors as it deems relevant. There is no assurance that the Fund's objective will be achieved. The Fund's allocations to the broad asset classes (equity, fixed income, and non-traditional) as set forth in the current Glidepath are not expected to vary from the Fund's allocations set forth in the strategic Glidepath by more than plus or minus 5%. The Fund's shareholder reports will set forth its actual allocations among asset classes and among Underlying Funds. The current Glidepath and strategic Glidepath are explained in more detail in the Fund's prospectus.

Quarterly Performance Attribution Highlights as of 12/31/2025

  • The fourth quarter was a positive one for the capital markets and for target date fund investors. U.S. stocks continued to rally upward and fixed income also delivered positive returns. On the back of solid capital market returns, PGIM Target Date Fund returns for the quarter ranged from 3.2% in the 2070 Fund to 1.3% in the Income Fund.
  • Relative to the shorter-dated funds in the series, higher equity exposure benefited the performance of the longer-dated funds (those furthest from their target retirement dates). Large-cap stocks (S&P 500 Index) delivered 2.7% for the period, slightly outpacing small cap stocks (Russell 2000: 2.2%). Non-U.S. stocks outperformed U.S. stocks, with MSCI EAFE and EM indexes returning 4.9% and 4.7%, respectively. The longer-dated funds in series not only have higher equity exposure than the shorter-dated funds but also have a higher percentage of total equity allocated to both small-cap U.S. stocks and EM stocks.
  • Bonds ended the quarter in positive territory, with the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index posting a 1.1% return. Inflation-protected bonds eked out a 0.1% fourth quarter return (Bloomberg U.S. TIPS Index). Relative to the longer-dated funds in the series, the shorter-dated funds have higher allocations to bonds, and a higher percentage of overall bond exposure allocated to TIPS.
  • Commodities proved beneficial to fund returns in the quarter, with the Bloomberg Commodity Index posting a 5.9% return, outperforming both stocks and bonds. Real estate, on the other hand, while playing an important role in portfolio diversification, delivered a negative return in the quarter (FTSE EPRA Nareit Developed Index: -0.7%).
  • The PGIM Target Date Funds had mixed results relative to their respective custom benchmarks in the fourth quarter, with the longer-dated funds outperforming their respective custom benchmarks and the shorter-dated funds underperforming. The PGIM Jennison Small Cap Core Equity Fund handily outperformed its benchmark, as did the PGIM Quant Solutions Emerging Markets Equity Fund, both of which were particularly additive to the longer-dated funds in the series, where there are higher allocations to small-cap and emerging markets equities. The PGIM Total Return Bond Fund (PTRQX) also outperformed its benchmark, while PGIM Global Real Estate (PGERX) and the PGIM Quant Solutions Commodity Strategies Funds lagged theirs.

PGIM Target Date Funds

Quarterly Economic Overview

The start of 2025 was marked by political transition and policy changes in Washington, but closed with discussions centered around AI, interest rate cuts, and labor market weakness. The Federal Reserve (Fed) cut interest rates three times in 2025, and while inflation remained stubbornly above its 2% target, growing strain in the labor market may justify further cuts in 2026. The FOMC dot plot shows expectations of one cut, but economists are projecting the possibility of multiple reductions. Despite hiring stagnation, layoffs remain fairly low and real wage growth is steady. PGIM's proprietary recession sentiment indicator developed by the Quantitative Solutions group shows reduced recession fears. The Fed's Q4 Survey of Professional Forecasters projects 1.8% GDP growth over the next year—slower, but not recessionary. Core CPI rose 2.7% year over year in November, coming in below the 3.1% forecast—a downside surprise that may have been distorted due to data disruptions stemming from the government shutdown. Unlike the U.S., the Eurozone is closer to its inflation target, allowing the European Central Bank greater flexibility to cut rates compared to the Fed. In contrast, the Bank of Japan (BoJ) hiked rates to 0.75% in December, as mounting inflation alongside strong GDP growth has set the stage for a hawkish shift in monetary policy. Nonetheless, the BoJ is not expected to hike again until at least the second half of 2026.

As 2026 begins, tariffs remain a key issue, with the potential to drive inflation higher and weigh on consumer spending. The Supreme Court is set to review the legality of 2025 tariffs, potentially invalidating more than 70% of them and causing fresh policy uncertainty, which could significantly alter trade dynamics. While a temporary U.S.-China trade truce is in place, elevated tariffs are dampening Chinese retail and industrial activity, posing risks to global growth. Despite a generally pro-risk macro backdrop, major leadership changes in France, the UK, and Japan have contributed to bond market volatility and heightened policy uncertainty across major economies.

Market Commentary

U.S. Equity

U.S. equities continued their advance in the fourth quarter of 2025, with the S&P 500 returning 2.7% on a total return basis, bringing the full-year 2025 return to 17.9%. Index performance was driven primarily by Communication Services (34%) and Information Technology (24%), but gains were broad-based, with all 11 sectors ending the year in positive territory. Technology and AI stocks led the market higher in 2025, with the Magnificent 7 accounting for an outsized share of U.S. equity market gains. Other asset classes outperformed U.S. equities, with international stocks gaining more than 30% and precious metals staging a strong rally to close the year up 80%. Nevertheless, U.S. equities continued to offer reasons to be bullish, with third-quarter earnings growth for the S&P 500 clocking in at a solid 8% year over year and 49% of companies issuing positive guidance. For the fourth quarter of 2025, large caps (S&P 500: 2.7%) slightly outperformed small caps (Russell 2000®: 2.2%) and midcaps (S&P MidCap 400: 1.6%). Over the past year, gains in large caps (S&P 500: 17.9%; 17.4%) outpaced those from midcaps (S&P MidCap 400: 7.5%) and small caps (Russell 2000®: 12.8%).

International Equity

International equities advanced in Q4 of 2025, with developed and emerging markets posting respective total returns of 4.9% and 4.7% in U.S. dollar terms. Developed markets saw broad-based gains, driven by a particularly strong quarter in Spain, while emerging markets were driven largely by South Korea. Technology and AI-focused companies fueled earnings growth in Asian markets, concentrated largely in the tech sector. For the full year, international equities emerged as the standout performers, benefiting from inflation concerns and geopolitical tensions, while U.S. stocks, particularly small caps, lagged. Against this backdrop, the MSCI EAFE Index posted a total return of 4.9% for the fourth quarter and a 12-month return of 31.2%. The MSCI Emerging Markets Index delivered net returns of 4.7% for Q4 and 33.6% over the full year (in U.S. dollar terms).

Non-Traditional

At the close of 2026, U.S. REITs look attractively priced, trading at about an 8.9% discount to NAV, which is significantly higher than the long-term average discount of 1-2%. Earnings growth may be expected to be strong through 2026, as supply/demand dynamics appear to support over 6.5% FFO growth. While still lagging the likely double-digit EPS growth expected by the broader S&P 500, the growth gap is narrowing. The Bloomberg Commodity Total Return Index increased by 5.6% in the fourth quarter, bringing it to up 15.8% for the full calendar year 2025. Returns in the quarter were driven by gains from Precious Metals (+22%) and Industrial Metals (+12%), while Energies and Softs declined over the quarter (-6% and -4%, respectively).

Fixed Income

The broad U.S. fixed income index continued to climb in Q4, building on upward momentum from earlier in 2025. U.S. Treasury yields were supported by three rate cuts in September, October, and December, as the FOMC determined that labor market weakness warranted easing despite sticky inflation. Chair Powell noted the Fed is now well positioned to “wait and see,” though several Fed governors have been vocal about the need for further cuts. For the quarter, bonds returned 1.1% on a total return basis as measured by the Bloomberg US Aggregate Bond Index. TIPS, as measured by the Bloomberg US Treasury TIPS Index, returned 0.1%. For the full calendar year 2025, the Bloomberg US Aggregate Bond Index returned 7.3%, and US TIPS rose 7.0%.

Performance Review: Quarterly Asset Allocation Effects

  • The fourth quarter was a positive one for the capital markets and for target date fund investors. U.S. stocks continued to rally upward and fixed income also delivered positive returns. On the back of solid capital market returns, PGIM Target Date Fund returns for the quarter ranged from 3.2% in the 2070 Fund to 1.3% in the Income Fund.
  • Relative to the shorter-dated funds in the series, higher equity exposure benefited the performance of the longer-dated funds (those furthest from their target retirement dates), as stocks outperformed bonds in the fourth quarter.
  • Large-cap stocks (S&P 500 Index) delivered 2.7% for the period, slightly outpacing small cap stocks (Russell 2000: 2.2%) Non-U.S. stocks outperformed U.S. stocks, with MSCI EAFE and EM indexes returning 4.9% and 4.7%, respectively. The longer-dated funds in series not only have higher equity exposure than the shorter-dated funds but also have higher percentage of total equity exposure allocated to both small-cap U.S. stocks and emerging markets stocks.
  • Bonds ended the quarter in positive territory, with the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index posting a 1.1% return. Inflation-protected bonds eked out a 0.1% fourth quarter return (Bloomberg U.S. TIPS Index). Relative to the longer-dated funds in the series, the shorter-dated funds have higher allocations to bonds, and a higher percentage of overall bond exposure allocated to TIPS.
  • Commodities proved beneficial to fund returns in the quarter, with the Bloomberg Commodity Index posting a 5.9% return, outperforming both stocks and bonds. Real estate, on the other hand, while playing an important role in portfolio diversification, delivered a negative return in the quarter (FTSE EPRA Nareit Developed Index: -0.7%).

Underlying Fund Gross Performance Commentary as of 12/31/2025

U.S. Equity

PGIM Quant Solutions Large-Cap Index Fund

  • The PGIM Quant Solutions Large-Cap Index Fund performed in line with its benchmark, the S&P 500 Index, in the fourth quarter.

PGIM Quant Solutions Mid-Cap Index Fund

  • The PGIM Quant Solutions Mid-Cap Index Fund performed in line with its benchmark, the S&P MidCap 400 Index, in the fourth quarter.

PGIM Jennison Small-Cap Core Equity Fund (PJSQX)

  • The PGIM Jennison Small-Cap Core Equity Fund significantly outperformed its benchmark, the Russell 2000 Index, in the fourth quarter.
  • Security selection within the information technology sector added the most value, driven by semiconductor holdings. Metals and mining positions drove outperformance in the materials sector. Consumer discretionary was the largest source of relative weakness due primarily to holdings in textiles apparel and luxury goods and automobile components.

International Equity

PGIM Quant Solutions International Developed Markets Index Fund

  • The PGIM Quant Solutions International Developed Markets Index Fund performed in line with the MSCI EAFE Index in the fourth quarter.

PGIM Quant Solutions Emerging Markets Equity Fund

  • The PGIM Quant Solutions Emerging Markets Equity Fund outperformed its benchmark, the MSCI Emerging Markets Index, in the fourth quarter.
  • By market, in relative terms, China (underweight in consumer discretionary and information technology) was the best performers followed by Mexico (led by overweight in materials), Korea (led by overweight/stock selection in information technology), and India (led by overweight in information technology). Factor performance was additive across all factor groups, with growth as the leading contributor. Top-down factors were additive during the quarter, as country-industries that the model identified as attractive outperformed.

Non-Traditional

PGIM Quant Solutions Commodity Strategies Fund

  • The PGIM Quant Solutions Commodity Strategies Fund lagged its benchmark index, the Bloomberg Commodity Index, in the fourth quarter.
  • Losses for the quarter were driven by underweights in silver and copper, which were partially offset by gains from an overweight in nickel. From a factor perspective, the carry and sentiment factors were the largest detractors while the risk factor added value.

PGIM Global Real Estate Fund

  • The PGIM Global Real Estate Fund performed in line with its benchmark, the FTSE EPRA/NAREIT Developed Real Estate Index, in the fourth quarter.
  • Positive contributions came from stock selection, while asset allocation detracted slightly from relative performance. In North America, outperformance was driven by strong stock selection in the healthcare, industrial, and residential sectors. Underweight allocations to office and gaming also supported relative returns. Canada contributed positively, primarily due to favorable stock selection. Europe weighed on overall results, driven primarily by weak security selection in the United Kingdom. The only notable contributor was positive stock selection in France. The Asia Pacific region underperformed for the period, with weak security selection in Japan, Australia, and Hong Kong driving the negative results.

Fixed Income

PGIM TIPS Fund (PTPQX)

  • The PGIM TIPS Fund lagged the Bloomberg U.S. TIPS Index for the fourth quarter.
  • Relative value trades marginally detracted from relative performance for the quarter. Yield curve positioning had a negligible impact, as did tactical duration management. The Fund ended the quarter with a neutral duration position versus the Index.

PGIM Core Conservative Bond Fund

  • The PGIM Core Conservative Bond Fund lagged its benchmark, the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index, for the fourth quarter.
  • While security selection in the MBS and U.S. investment grade corporate sectors contributed the most to returns relative to the index, selection within the emerging markets sector detracted. During the quarter, the Fund's yield curve and duration positioning had a muted impact on relative results.

PGIM Total Return Bond Fund

  • The PGIM Total Return Bond Fund outperformed its benchmark, the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index, in the fourth quarter.
  • Overall security selection was the largest contributor to returns during the period, with selection in U.S. investment grade corporates, U.S. high yield corporates, and AAA collateralized loan obligations ('CLO') contributing the most. This was partly offset by selection in emerging-market high yield bonds and non-agency commercial mortgage-backed securities ('CMBS') AA-and-below, which detracted. Overall sector allocation also contributed to performance, with overweights to the emerging-market high yield and CLO AAA sectors, along with an underweight to the U.S. investment grade corporate sector, contributing the most. On average, having more market risk in the Fund than the benchmark contributed positively to results during the period. In aggregate, the Fund's duration and yield curve positioning contributed slightly to performance as front-end yields on the U.S. Treasury curve declined.

References

  1. The PGIM Quant Solutions Large-Cap Index Fund was previously named the PGIM Quant Solutions Stock Index Fund; the PGIM Target Date Funds began allocating to the Fund effective 12/11/23.
  2. Prior to 12/11/23, the PGIM Quant Solutions Mid-Cap Index fund was named the PGIM Quant Solutions Mid-Cap Core Fund; also effective 12/11/23, the Fund discontinued its active core mandate and began pursuing a passively managed mandate, seeking to track the performance of the S&P MidCap 400 Index.
  3. Effective 12/11/23, the PGIM Quant Solutions International Developed Markets Index Fund's benchmark was changed from the FTSE Developed Markets ex North America Net Index to the MSCI EAFE Net Index.

data.title: RSBT ETF: It's A Directional Bet, Not A Diversification Vehicle
data.description:
It's time to look at a potential diversification strategy by assessing the Return Stacked Bonds & Managed Futures ETF (RSBT). Various pivots have occurred in the global macro environment during recent years, and nearer-term reversions have shown signs of resurgence, making it an excellent time to review a vehicle such as RST, which has delivered price returns of ~12.25% year-over-year and currently has a trailing dividend yield of around 2.99%.

Herewith is my forward-looking take on the RSBT ETF.

What Is RSBT?

Figure 1 explains RSBT's strategy, which is to split roughly half of its portfolio into iShares Core US Aggregate Bond ETF (AGG), while investing the other half of its capital into managed futures strategies with the hope of producing higher risk-adjusted returns at an expense ratio of approximately 1.02% - distributions have been in the past, although sustainability beckons (distribution history available via this link). For those unaware, AGG ETF is an aggregate U.S. bond ETF weighted on supply; its current exposure includes: ~46% Treasuries, ~24% agency securities, +20% in financial & industrial corporate issuers, and residual allocations in sectors such as CMBS and utility issuers.

According to RSBT's material, its futures concept relies on external trend-following strategies. As a personal opinion, I can tell you that I've gained exposure to trend following during academia and later in-practice; futures can present momentum for a sustained period, but some have strong mean-reverting properties. Moreover, a futures book's macro exposure can be closely linked to your collateral base's macro exposure, which is self-defeating if your goal is diversification.

Figure 3 illustrates the fund's futures product universe. The selection pool is very streamlined, likely due to liquidity preferences. Ideally, I would've preferred seeing the fund exploit mispricing via long/short strategies as opposed to relying on trend. Long-Short relative value in futures can remove the macro-linkage between the underlying portfolio and the futures book. As things stand, I think trend following will expand the fund's beta as opposed to reducing it.

Figure 4 presents a view of the vehicle's current holdings. It's revealed that RPBT has long positions across the Treasury curve. Moreover, long exposure is held to second month Euro futures, Australian Dollar Futures, FTSE 100 futures, and S&P 500 E-Mini contracts. As previously mentioned, the fund's exposure changes rather frequently.

Lastly for this section, a time series of returns shows 12.25% in year-over-year gains and a cumulative loss of ~1.74% since-inception decline.

My Outlook

Headline & Correlation

The ETF's holdings aren't stagnant. However, as a means of illustration, I used its 2025 year-end reported holdings and created a pairwise analysis to assess correlation and covariance time-series - here's a list of the assets that I included (note that they were not weighted).

I started by forming a vector, where I ran a pairwise correlation and found a current 52-week rolling correlation of 0.34. Asset correlations broke down during 2025, presumably due to outliers, namely gold's illustrious run. Nonetheless, it can be seen from a throughout-the-cycle analysis that the positions rarely clustered. In fact, clusters during the pandemic and 2022's bear market didn't result in intra-asset values correlating anywhere near 1:1. While RSBT's holdings might change in time, I think we could reasonably conclude that the vehicle doesn't hold pure systematic exposure to a single asset class.

Assigning values to the vectors through covariances shows that volatility can cluster more vigorously than correlation in itself, proven by the pandemic. The covariance doesn't disprove correlation benefits, but it does warrant attention.

A direct reference to RSBT ETF's time series returns shows that, despite attempts at diversification, the managed futures strategy didn't add downside protection against the 2022/23 inflation-driven bond sell-off. Instead, returns decayed in excess (versus AGG). In contrast, we saw the vehicle outperform the AGG ETF strongly this year amid a rebound in bonds and outlying gains in precious metals.

What am I getting at?

I'm trying to say that we are likely looking at a higher beta version of the AGG ETF, which I suspect might be because of the vehicle's futures diversification strategy. It invests in a broad range of asset classes, but they are all macro-linked. As an example, the ETF's current futures portfolio (figure 4) basically bets on the same yield curve as the underlying bonds in the AGG ETF. The index positions in equities will likely add beta and not diversify during crisis regimes. FX and commodity diversification can soften downside beta but also lead to crash risk, seeing as the ETF's managed futures strategies are trend-following.

Assessing Its Hybrid Features

RSBT ETF's promotional material suggests that managed futures be held as a hybrid, by dividing a 60/40s bond exposure into 20% fixed income, 20% managed futures or by replacing bonds with managed futures entirely. While I can see the logic, I will highlight this: Past returns show that the RSBT ETF trades like a high-beta bond more than a portfolio convexity enhancer. Moreover, RSBT relies on trend-following in domains such as rates and FX, which are, by nature, mean-reverting. In my view, sticking to equity and precious metals trend-following would present better results. Alternatively, one could opt for relative value or discretionary strategies within asset classes, where you don't extract macro risk correlated to the underlying portfolio or run into crash risk issues.

My Directional Outlook (Based On Current Holdings)

I can only speak on behalf of the fund's current holdings when I provide a directional view. Based on my observations, I conclude the following.

Rates: I support the vehicle's current positioning in rates, where it holds a bullish view of Treasury futures in various parts of the curve. As communicated in my recent analysis of the iShares 20+ Year Treasury Bond ETF (TLT), I expect the yield curve to level down, providing support throughout the curve.

AUD/USD: The Australian dollar has delivered a ~10%+ return year-over-year versus the U.S. Dollar, largely on the basis of a rise in commodity prices. I see coin-integration with RSBT ETF's gold strategy in this trade. However, I think we could see foreign investors sustain mild momentum in the Australian dollar via short-term Carry trades; Australia has entered a re-inflationary trend, and its benchmark rates are currently at 385 basis points versus U.S. target rates of 350 - 375 basis points, and UK benchmark rates of 375 basis points (the UK has also entered disinflation).

FTSE & U.S. Equities: We are seeing sector rotation and stretched multiples in the S&P 500 and a trend from the FTSE 100. I'm not overly bullish or bearish about equity markets. However, I would highlight that they correlate during bad times, and although I acknowledge the strength of the momentum anomaly, I do not like the idea of trend-following at this time - regime shifts are occurring in equities - I cite "the great rotation."

I don't have directional opinions of the other asset classes available in RSBT ETF's selected pool. As a few mild observations, I believe the Euro to be a solid low-volatility currency alternative to the U.S. Dollar, but I don't have a directional view on it at the moment. I have a bullish long-run view of precious metals, but returns can be lumpy, and therefore, I don't like gold or silver as a managed futures strategy, at this stage.

As for asset classes that might be included ex-ante. I believe we could see Crude Oil trend-following being included in due course, given the multi-month rise in prices if both WTI and Brent. However, I don't like trading oil on trends, I prefer discretionary views, relative value, or spread trading, as a means of lowering portfolio beta and enhancing alpha.

Other Mentions

Dividends

Distributions have occurred in the past but details about future distribution plans remain sparse. I don't see this as a reliable proxy for AGG ETF, if the aim is to generate income.

Regime

I assessed the vehicle on a relatively short timeframe due to the vehicle's relatively short trading history. Since listing, the ETF has faced significant volatility from exogenous events, such as a 2022/23 sovereign debt market sell-off, a 2022 equity bear market and USD debasement. I think we'll see a few more regime changes in the next few years, which may present differentiated results from what we've seen from the RSBT ETF until now.

Conclusion

An analysis of the Return Stacked Bonds & Managed Futures ETF shows that it remains relatively cyclical, despite the correlation benefits derived from its managed futures strategy. Covariance can spike in certain regimes, largely because the ETF's managed futures overlay has similar macro-exposure as the fund's underlying position in AGG ETF, as opposed to style-based or discretionary exposure. Furthermore, the ETF exploits trend-following strategies, which can deliver robust returns for a sustained period. That being said, its trend following is exposed to mean reversion in rates and FX as well as crash risk in equities and precious metals.

Am I considering the RSBT ETF at this stage? Not quite; I don't see clear convexity gains from its strategy, mostly due to the reasons mentioned above. I think conviction in Treasuries, Agency Securities, and relative value futures would provide better portfolio risk utility than a trend follower with macro linkages between the underlying exposure and the futures overlay.

With all that being said, RSBT ETF has delivered stellar year-over-year returns. So, I might revisit my stance in the near future.

data.title: U.S. Goods Trade Deficit Hits All-Time High In 2025 - What Does It Mean For Q4 GDP?
data.description:
US trade deficit continued to widen in December after hitting the smallest monthly amount since the 2009 low in October. The total import was USD 70.3 billion higher than the export in the last month of 2025, according to the US Census Bureau.

Period 2025-12-01 2025-11-01 2025-10-01 2025-09-01 2025-08-01 2025-07-01 2025-06-01
Trade Balance -70.3 -53.0 -28.7 -47.7 -55.2 -73.9 -57.7
Exports 287.3 292.3 302.6 293.9 283.7 283.6 280.2
Imports 357.6 345.3 331.3 341.6 338.9 357.5 337.9

Now we have a full picture of the accumulated trade balance in 2025: compared to 2024, the full-year trade deficit for goods and services was merely USD 2.06 billion lower. The shortfall was unusually concentrated in the first half of the year as firms rushed to stock up on foreign products before the tariff policies hit.

If we zoom in to just goods trade, the accumulated goods trade gap rose to USD 1,230 billion in 2025, from USD 1,204 billion in the previous year, reaching a new all-time high.

What do these trade data mean for Q4 GDP

The December figures also give us a complete overview of Q4 net export contribution to economic growth. Now, to translate trade numbers into GDP contributions, one of the most important adjustments these days is to first exclude non-monetary gold.

This is because most of the cross-border trade involving non-monetary gold involves no local production in the US; they are merely ownership transfers of investment assets that are not within GDP’s coverage.

Removing non-monetary gold from trade data, the trade deficit for goods and services in December would rise to USD 73 billion.

Take the average of the month of ex-gold trade deficit in Q4 (shown in the red lines in the graph below), and we can see the number was very close to the level in Q3. This means we should expect a minimal contribution from net exports in the Q4 GDP.

This is also the same conclusion the Atlanta Fed’s GDPNow draws. The nowcasting tool degraded its Q4 GDP growth estimate to 3%, with the expected contribution from net exports falling to merely 0.02 percentage points (pps), from 0.62 pps in the second most recent update.

data.title: FX Outlook: USD/CHF Carves A Bottom After Reaching 14-Year Lows
data.description:
By Elior Manier

2025 was the year of the Swiss franc, and there were quite a few reasons. De-dollarization, flows moving towards Europe, the yen losing some of its safe-haven characteristics amid fiscal trouble in Japan, and general diversification towards quality as the world faces troubled times ahead.

Reaching 14-year lows less than a month ago, USD/CHF had become a bear dream for those who thrive on dollar outflows. 2025 began with a 17% decline in the major pair and was at the center of the essential themes driving FX flows.

But as dollar bearish positioning also reaches decade lows, some questions regarding the extended moves are arising. If the US dollar finds reasons to catch a serious bid, troubled times could be coming ahead for heavy Greenback-Short sellers. A question evoked in our recent dollar index analysis.

Switzerland is a victim of quite heavy deflationary pressures, with its currency strengthening (hurting Swiss exports) and the rougher US trade policies, which exacerbate a fundamentally low-inflation regime.

The latest inflation report showed a modest 0.1% increase, but if inflation doesn't show a material increase in the coming few reports, the Central Bank might be forced to turn to negative rates, and such come at high costs.

In the meantime, USD/CHF has carved out a pretty strong bottom in recent days and could offer interesting mean-reversion setups for those looking for FX volatility ahead.

Let's dive right into a multi-timeframe analysis of USD/CHF to spot where the action stands and where it could be heading.

USD/CHF Multi-Timeframe Technical Analysis

Daily Chart

The daily RSI and uptrend are working together to provide a fresh push in the pair, with traders leaning on the Monday lows (0.7675) to drive the action higher.

  • If the action falls back below the weekly lows, the breakout will be void but for now it gives high probabilities of holding.

4H Chart and Technical Levels

Trading Levels for USD/CHF

Resistance Levels

  • 0.7780 to 0.78 momentum pivot
  • 0.7850 2025 lows pivotal resistance (bullish above)
  • 0.7950 minor resistance
  • 0.8075 to 0.81 late 2025 range highs

Support Levels

  • 0.7725 50H moving average
  • 0.77 to 0.7725 August 2011 lows support
  • 0.76292 2026 and 14-year lows
  • 0.76 support zone July 2011
  • 0.70696 all-time lows (August 2011)

data.title: Small Caps Stage Quiet Comeback As AI Trade Shows Cracks
data.description:
By Jim Iuorio

After years in the shadow of big tech, small-cap stocks are outperforming, and the reasons suggest the trend may have staying power. The Russell 2000's four-month outperformance against mega-cap tech represents one of 2025's most significant but underappreciated market rotations.

In November, concerns around stretched valuations began to grow, and on November 20, E-mini Nasdaq-100 (NQ) options volume marked their third highest volume day as traders hedged and navigated tech uncertainty following Nvidia earnings and Non-Farm Payrolls (NFP) data.

Since November 21, the small-cap Russell 2000 index has surged more than 16% and touched record highs on January 22. Over the same period, the Nasdaq climbed just 8%. The S&P 500 hasn't kept pace either, despite reaching its own new highs in late January.

The shift marks a striking reversal from the previous three years, when artificial intelligence (AI) euphoria drove the Nasdaq up 145% while small caps languished. To fully understand the mechanics of this trade, let’s go back to the beginning.

Years of Divergence

In early 2023, the Nasdaq Composite began what would become a historic rally, surging 145% in less than three years. The advance was remarkable not just for its magnitude, but for its narrow leadership. A small group of mega-cap technology companies, particularly those at the forefront of the AI evolution, drove the bulk of the gains.

To illustrate the magnitude of this move: When the Nasdaq reached its all-time high in November 2023, the S&P 500 trailed far behind with a gain of 79%, and the Russell 2000 lagged even further behind with a modest 43% gain. The tailwind for the Nasdaq was widely reported and dominated financial media headlines, while Russell's underperformance received less attention.

The Case Against Small Caps

The fundamental case for small-cap underperformance was multifaceted and, for a time, compelling. The most pressing concern centered on interest rates. In January 2023, the Federal Reserve's benchmark rate stood at 4.25%, already elevated by recent historical standards. But with inflation still running hot, markets expected further tightening – and got it. By July 2023, the Fed had raised rates to 5.25%, adding 100 basis points of pressure.

Stocks included in the Russell tend to be extremely sensitive to short-term rates. Many of these smaller companies use revolving rates to conduct business, unlike cash-rich entities like Apple (AAPL) and Google (GOOG) (GOOGL).

Economic uncertainty added another layer of concern. A disappointing 1.3% GDP reading in December 2022 sparked widespread fears of an imminent recession. Although those fears would prove premature – the recession never materialized – the anxiety lingered for several quarters, keeping investors cautious about economically sensitive small-cap stocks.

This did not seem to stop traders from favoring the Nasdaq over the Russell. Even when the Fed pivoted to cutting rates in the fall of 2024, delivering 100 basis points of easing, the outperformance of the Nasdaq persisted.

By then, the narrative appeared to have shifted. Rate sensitivity mattered less than the seemingly unstoppable momentum behind artificial intelligence and the mega-cap stocks positioned to capitalize on it.

A Change in Conditions

By late 2025, however, three significant developments began to alter the equation. First, the Fed resumed its easing cycle with 75 basis points of cuts. What makes these rate reductions particularly notable is their timing: they are occurring against a backdrop of economic expansion, not weakness.

The Fed's focus appears to be the labor market, where recent data have shown softening despite otherwise healthy economic indicators. Under its dual mandate of price stability and maximum employment, the central bank is responding to labor market weakness even as other measures suggest the economy remains on solid footing.

Second, the Fed appears to be anticipating that the same AI that’s eliminating jobs might also combat inflation through productivity gains. If true, 2026 could bring a Goldilocks scenario of moderate economic growth, low unemployment and stable inflation – especially with potential tailwinds from tax cuts, deregulation and reshoring investment. Small caps are more sensitive to these domestic factors, as they are less likely than large caps to be doing business across the globe.

Third, AI exuberance itself is showing signs of fatigue. Nvidia (NVDA) shed 20% in early November, and the entire Mag 7 lost 10%. While these are minor moves when viewed against their enormous multi-year rallies, they raise a critical question: Has this combination of factors provided enough incentive to cause a rush of liquidation in the long Nasdaq/short Russell trade?

If the answer is yes, and that liquidation has indeed begun, it suggests a path for continued outperformance of the Russell into 2026.

Accessing the Trade

For investors interested in expressing this view, pair trading with Equity Index options enables traders to express relative performance views between indices while maintaining defined risk parameters regardless of overall market direction.

The strategy's effectiveness hinges on thoughtful position construction, where traders must choose between dollar-neutral weighting – which equalizes notional exposure – or volatility-adjusted weighting that accounts for the historical behavior differences between indices like the Russell 2000 and Nasdaq-100.

By utilizing liquid CME Group Equity options contracts, market participants can structure trades that isolate their conviction about one index outperforming another, transforming directional market views into actionable positions with clear maximum loss thresholds.

data.title: Annaly Capital's Easy Money Is Gone - But The 12% Yield Isn't
data.description:
In May 2025, I had rated Annaly Capital Management (NLY) as a Hold, leaning toward a Buy (Hold for the short term, accumulation for long term investors). I did see positives ahead, as yield curve steepening would improve spreads and dividend durability looked intact. As things transpired, markets discounted a recovering macro environment ahead of time, and May of last year was indeed the best time to buy NLY, even from a short-term lens. NLY retained its high-yield profile over the next few quarters and importantly, has seen ~33% total returns too (price appreciation of ~20%).

With an economic return of ~8% in Q4 alone, NLY closed a great year in 2025, where economic returns were above 20%. Q4 also saw a 5% sequential rise in book value. EAD was again sufficient to cover a lucrative yield of above 12% (annualizing ~$0.7 in dividends paid in Q4). Even economic leverage is conservative at ~5.6x. Overall, the inflection I thought would take some time but emerge over a longer term, has arrived earlier than expected.

The forward question changes, though. The markets seem to have priced in the inflection to a large extent. With spreads already tight and valuations now at a premium (was trading at a discount in May last year), NLY appears to be a Hold with most of the gains ahead driven by the income component rather than book value expansion or further valuation rerating.

Spread Environment

The Fed cut rates by 75 bps in 2025, and short-term borrowing costs declined. This meant Annaly's average repo rate also improved by 30 bps to ~4.2% for the full year (4.02% in Q4). So, in 2025, net interest spread (ex-PAA) was roughly 1.4%, up by almost 18 bps. These are the basic mechanics explaining the solid EAD covering a lucrative dividend and a strong full year economic return, we noted earlier. Additionally, 2025 also meant lower rate volatility, lower hedging costs, and tighter mortgage spreads, helping lift asset prices and hence a stronger book value.

The current environment is stable but does not seem to indicate the same kind of inflection ahead. Repo costs remain stable near ~4%, supported by improved funding conditions. But Agency spreads are now near the tighter end of their historical range. That means the large tailwind from spread compression has already occurred. Which is why I feel this is an environment where carry stability will dominate NLY's thesis, not another round of repricing. According to management commentary in Q4 2025,

So the Agency market is a safe place right now. It's just that spreads are obviously at the tight end of the range. They're close to QE type levels. The safety of those returns is there, but the abundance of yield is not quite there.

Dividend Anchored in Spread Economics

For long-term investors, it is important to understand that structurally NLY's dividend policy implies a huge dependency on a supportive spread regime (with a ~95% payout ratio). The yield therefore fluctuates significantly, and payouts could be revised, particularly if the EAD drops structurally (not a single quarter temporary fall). If funding costs rise or Agency spreads compress further from already tight levels, EAD could decline.

There is no immediate cause of visible concern, though. With leverage at a moderate 5.6x and liquidity representing roughly 58% of total capital, the current balance sheet does not indicate stress. Management also stated they expect to out-earn the dividend in Q1, which supports near-term stability.

Portfolio Shift Indicates Stability

The dividend yield variability tied to a cyclical Agency spread environment is likely to be addressed to an extent by NLY's portfolio shift toward more Residential Credit and MSR (which will reduce the Agency mix from 62% to 50%). This should smooth cash flows and provide a return stream that is more diversified away from Agency MBS tightening. These newer areas of emphasis introduce different variables like borrower quality and servicing behavior and reduce NLY's EAD dependence on funding spreads and rate volatility alone.

On the credit side, this expansion looks cushioned with disciplined underwriting (762 FICO, 68% LTV) rather than yield chasing. In the MSR portfolio too, the current position appears healthy. With mortgage rates well above the portfolio's 3.28% weighted average note rate, borrowers are roughly 250 bps out of the money. That means refinancing incentives are low. As a result, prepayment risks are lower (CPR at 4.6%), and servicing fees should continue to add up and keep EAD steadily supported. In this setup, MSR behaves like a stable, recurring income stream. This ensures that a tight spread in the Agency segment is not the central point of focus.

MSR is not designed to drive higher earnings in favorable spread environments, but to provide balance - offering steady income when rates are high and acting as a partial hedge against Agency convexity risk when rates fall - smoothing performance across different rate regimes.

Overall, the portfolio shifts are not necessarily EAD-boosting. Agency assets, when spreads are favorable and leverage is applied, can generate stronger immediate carry. So, emphasis on MSR and Residential Credit is more about smoother than larger EAD ahead. This does not ensure dividends are protected, but fluctuations will be far fewer.

Valuation and Risks

Having discussed the business' health, let us now turn our attention to the stock. Compared to May last year, the most significant change for the stock has been a rerating toward historically high premiums in terms of price-to-book value. The high valuations coincide with an environment where risks are not at their lowest. Management highlighted risks from a global debt complacency and asset valuation euphoria. A G-fee cut could also impact the prepayment environment.

Looking at the balance of valuations and risks at play today (valuations are more sensitive to shocks now), I feel NLY should be best treated as a Hold and viewed as a carry position, where most of the heavy lifting ahead is done by the yield. The current indicators do not show any potential and immediate stress on the EAD.

Long-term investors can continue to Hold NLY and keep an eye on Agency MBS spread widening, any macro signs of repo stress, rate volatility, and even intra-quarter book value updates, if any, to trim positions. Short term investors, who do not have the outlook to ride full economic cycles, will have to take valuations into account as well. There too, current valuations do not support fresh capital deployment. So unless you are already invested, it is best to wait for share prices to return toward book value, or for a spread widening environment that improves asset entry yields.

data.title: FXR: Sophisticated Strategy With A High Fee Lagging XLI, A Hold
data.description:
I believe that while this year's capital rotation to cyclicals is a phenomenon that is impossible to ignore, the First Trust Industrials/Producer Durables AlphaDEX® Fund ETF (FXR) is a suboptimal vehicle to bet on this shift to continue further into 2026 and thus reap benefits from industrials' rally.

My stance is driven by the fact that FXR has a history of consistent underperformance not only vs. the iShares Core S&P 500 ETF (IVV) but also vs. the State Street® Industrial Select Sector SPDR® ETF (XLI), which is quite disappointing assuming substantial differences in their expense ratios and that FXR's strategy incorporates sophisticated screening and factor considerations. And while this ETF does have a few advantages over XLI (chiefly in terms of value factor exposure), I believe it does not qualify for a Buy rating.

FXR Strategy And Portfolio

As we know from the First Trust website, incepted in 2007, FXR tracks the StrataQuant® Industrials Index. The index calibration process is rather sophisticated, with multiple steps involved. As explained in the fact sheet, the process starts with the Russell 1000® Index:

IDI constructs the StrataQuant® Industrials Index by ranking the stocks which are members of the Russell 1000® Index on growth factors including three, six and 12-month price appreciation, sales to price and one year sales growth, and, separately, on value factors including book value to price, cash flow to price and return on assets. All stocks are ranked on the sum of ranks for the growth factors and, separately, all stocks are ranked on the sum of ranks for the value factors.

In the next step, it is decided whether a stock belongs to a value or a growth cohort or is something in-between:

Each stock receives either its growth or value score rank as its selection score based on its style designation as determined by Russell. Stocks which Russell allocates between both growth and value receive the better of their growth or value score rank as their selection score.

After that, names with the highest scores from the respective sector join the index:

IDI then ranks those stocks contained in the industrials/producer durables sector according to their score. The greater of the top 75% of the eligible universe or 40 stocks is selected for the StrataQuant® Industrials Index.

Ultimately, I should also highlight that the weighting scheme applied is also far from simple:

The selected stocks are divided into quintiles based on their rankings and the top ranked quintiles receive a higher weight within the index. The stocks are equally-weighted within each quintile.

So given such rigorous screening and complicated weighting, I am not surprised that FXR has a 76% turnover, especially considering the index is not just rebalanced but also reconstituted quarterly. To contextualize, XLI's turnover is just 3%.

FXR vs. XLI: Portfolio Composition

What is inside the FXR portfolio, and does it demonstrate exposures compelling enough to favor it over XLI? I have identified a few advantages it does offer, mainly in terms of the value factor, yet they are not strong enough to justify a bullish stance.

More specifically, my analysis shows that the product of this complex methodology is a portfolio of 145 equities that has an overlap with XLI but not the one I would call substantial, as only 30.37% of its net assets are allocated to companies that qualified for the Industrial Select Sector Index (hence, for XLI). Interestingly, in the XLI portfolio, these names play a much greater role, accounting for 67% of the net assets. And this is not the consequence of just rigorous factor screening.

The issue here is more nuanced: all the stocks present in the FXR portfolio belong to the Industrials industry (and the seven sectors within it, from aerospace and defense to industrial transportation) in the Industry Classification Benchmark system, which is maintained by FTSE Russell. But the methodology the Industrial Select Sector index relies on is the Global Industry Classification Standard, which is maintained by MSCI Inc. (MSCI). And when the GICS is applied to the FXR portfolio, we can, to our surprise, find out that there are five sectors in it instead of one: it also features consumer discretionary, financials, IT, and materials.

Sector Weight, as of February 13
Industrials 67.27%
Materials 16.31%
Financials 8.82%
Information Technology 4.22%
Consumer Discretionary 3.27%

Calculated by the author using data from FXR and Seeking Alpha

Looking at the key ten group with Comfort Systems USA, Inc. at the very top, we see that the similarities between it and the top 10 cohort of XLI are scarce.

Company Ticker Weight in FXR, as of February 13 Weight in XLI, as of February 13 EV/EBITDA TTM GICS Sector Market Cap, $ Billion, as of February 17
