GE Vernova Inc. (GEV) 1.25% 4.07% 69.98 Industrials 216.20

Created by the author using data from the ETFs and Seeking Alpha

For instance, five names have not qualified for the XLI portfolio, given they are not S&P 500 constituents (but they must be to join the index XLI tracks), while DD did not make it to the index as it is a materials company in the GICS system. And Caterpillar Inc. (CAT), one of the leaders in the construction machinery & heavy transportation equipment industry, which is the most significant holding of XLI with around 6.8% allocated, is only in 20th place in FXR, having just 1.1% weight.

What we also see in the table above is that a few of these names have quite stretched EV/EBITDA (Trailing Twelve Months) ratios—for the industrials sector, the median multiple is 14.96x, and for materials, it is even lower at 12.1x.

Still, this is just the first impression, and deeper research does not confirm the expensiveness of FXR's portfolio.

As we can immediately tell from FXR's over 27% allocation to stocks with a B- Quant Valuation rating or higher, its portfolio is, in fact, much less expensive than those of XLI and IVV. The same message is conveyed by the adjusted weighted average earnings yield and other metrics. One the contributors here is that FXR has considerably larger exposure to SMID stocks (names with market capitalizations below $10 billion), with close to a third of the net assets allocated to them.

Next, growth, which is, by and large, the antipode of value, is where the FXR portfolio is noticeably weaker than both these names.

Interestingly, FXR is also underweight in momentum names (those with a Quant Momentum grade of B- or higher).

As to the GARP characteristics, the picture is mixed, as FXR has a lower PEG ratio (calculated with the weighted average TTM P/E in the numerator and forward EPS growth rate in the denominator) than XLI, but its EV/EBITDA-to-Growth ratio is nonetheless larger.

Metric FXR XLI IVV
PEG Ratio 2.264 2.522 1.239
Price/Sales-to-Growth Ratio 0.533 0.615 0.539
EV/EBITDA-to-Growth Ratio 2.260 2.136 1.043

Calculated by the author using data from Seeking Alpha and the ETFs

And compared to IVV, both its PEG and EV/EBITDA-to-Growth ratios are substantially higher, while the PSG ratios are on par.

Regarding quality, I would not say that the sophisticated selection and weighting methodology of the index FXR tracks translated here into meaningful advantages over XLI, which has a much simpler approach. It still has a higher adjusted Return on Equity (with negative and triple-digit figures removed), yet its allocation to stocks with a B- Quant Profitability rating or higher, a parameter I prioritize over individual weighted average metrics, is the smallest in the group.

Importantly, even if the index that lies at the crux of XLI applied an equal weighting scheme instead of the modified market cap weighting, the portfolio's allocation to stocks that earned no less than a B- Profitability grade would be close to 95%, as my model shows.

FXR Performance

With a 60 bps expense ratio, FXR is anything but an inexpensive vehicle. For context, XLI has an ER of just 8 bps. Besides, I did further research on whether expenses were even higher in the past, and using the Wayback Machine that helped me to find the version of its web page saved on August 15, 2007, I found out that back then (and FXR was incepted in May 2007), its gross ER was 0.89% and the net ER was 0.70%.

But considering expenses that high, investors might expect encouraging performance. And here, there is a problem: beating XLI, the iShares Russell 1000 ETF (IWB), and IVV has been quite problematic for FXR (except for quite rare periods, including YTD).

Optimists can make a point though that this year FXR has been doing excellently, with a price return above those of XLI, the iShares Core S&P 500 ETF, and IWB.

Next, with 18 full calendar months in the books, FXR outperformed XLI in 9, which is a decent result on the surface.

However, delving deeper, we see that FXR has still underperformed XLI (which, by the way, has recently closed the performance gap with the S&P 500 ETF) by over 131% since its inception in May 2007, also trailing IVV and IWB.

Besides, my calculations show that in its 9 successful calendar years, the median active return (with XLI selected as a benchmark) was 4.3% vs. -5.46% in the weak years.

So, for the June 2007–January 2026 period, it delivered an annualized total return 1.38% lower than XLI's and 1.72% lower than IVV's, capturing close to 131% of the downside of the latter (and 110.68% of the former), while also delivering the highest standard deviation in the group.

Final Thoughts

Even though FXR is beating the State Street® Industrial Select Sector SPDR® ETF this year, given its history of underperformance, deep maximum drawdowns, substantially higher expense ratio, and a 10x larger 30-day median bid/ask spread (which was at 0.10% as of February 18), it is quite complicated to justify why investors should favor it over XLI, regardless of whether they are going to tactically cut exposure to the tech-heavy S&P 500 ETF to play the capital rotation narrative or find a vehicle for holding long-term with a strategic purpose in mind. In this regard, while I find FXR's factor mix quite compelling in terms of value factor exposure and the strategy certainly sensible, I would gravitate toward a neutral view, which equals the Hold rating.

data.title: Fortress Biotech: The PRV Catalyst That Could Reprice This Microcap
data.description:
Fortress Biotech (FBIO) is a company that mostly acquires and develops drugs and biologic treatments through subsidiaries, partner companies, and external collaborations. FBIO's dermatology assets are developed through Journey Medical (DERM), which markets a set of branded products like the recently approved Emrosi for rosacea. FBIO retains its longer-dated optionality across late-stage and earlier programs through equity and royalties. This way, FBIO still has exposure to diverse areas like oncology, rare diseases, infectious illnesses, transplant immunology, pain treatments, and metabolic conditions. More importantly, in January 2026, they secured a valuable PRV, which I feel could quickly unlock significant shareholder value. Thus, I feel FBIO's risk-reward at this point now favors the bulls at these levels.

Dermatology And Rollup M&A

Fortress Biotech is, at its core, a life sciences company that works on dermatology treatments. However, they also acquire other drug and biologic programs in multiple therapeutic areas to advance them through partnerships and subsidiaries. FBIO was founded back in 2006 and is currently headquartered in Bay Harbor Islands, Florida. Having said that, FBIO's business model differs from most other biotechs as it's explicitly focused on trying to acquire late-stage assets or already approved drugs in foreign jurisdictions. So, in that sense, FBIO is not a pure play on biotech R&D but rather a bet on management's insights on the state of the art in biotech and their ability to execute on their acquired candidates.

As a quick overview, note that FBIO's portfolio includes Emrosi, which is a tetracycline-class oral therapy for inflammatory lesions of rosacea in adults. This product was developed by Journey Medical, a partner focused on dermatology, owned 37% by FBIO. Other dermatological offerings in FBIO's portfolio are Amzeeq and Zilxi, also for rosacea, acquired from VYNE Therapeutics Inc. (VYNE), and Qbrexza, indicated for hyperhidrosis, acquired from Dermira, an Eli Lilly (LLY) subsidiary. Additionally, the following drugs are commercialized by DERM: Accutane and Targadox for acne, Exelderm for fungal and dermatological infections, and Luxamend for wound and skin care.

Likewise, FBIO owns CUTX-101, which is another commercial product (also known as Zycubo). This is a copper replacement therapy for Menkes disease in pediatric patients. The FDA approved CUTX-101 on January 13, 2026, and it was initially developed by FBIO's subsidiary Cyprium Therapeutics. Later, Cyprium completed an asset transfer to Sentynl, which is how they retain 3.0%-12.5% of royalties and other participations from this particular asset.

Late Stage Pipeline

Moreover, it's important to highlight that FBIO's pipeline has several late-stage candidates. One of them is CAEL-101 (anselamimab), a monoclonal antibody studied in Phase 3 for AL amyloidosis therapy by Caelum. This company was a former subsidiary of FBIO that was acquired by AstraZeneca (AZN) in 2021. And FBIO can eventually receive up to $125 million after reaching certain R&D and commercial milestones. Unfortunately, its recent Phase 3 trial didn't actually achieve statistical significance for the primary endpoint in the overall population. FBIO believes there are still some subgroup signals that remain promising and are under evaluation, but it's clearly no longer a major value driver for FBIO at this point.

Another one of FBIO's late-stage candidates is Dotinurad, which is a uric acid transporter 1 inhibitor, used to lower serum uric acid for gout and hyperuricemia. This asset is much more promising, as it's already approved in some Asian countries and has advanced into global Phase 3. Dotinurad was developed by Urica Therapeutics, a company majority-owned by FBIO. Urica sold Dotinurad to Crystalys Therapeutics in 2024, but FBIO retains upside exposure through a minority position in Crystalys. Plus, FBIO remains eligible for 3% on royalty payments contingent on future net sales of Dotinurad.

Likewise, another late-stage asset is Triplex. This one is a viral-vector vaccine designed to generate T-cell responses against cytomegalovirus (CMV) complications, which is especially important in immunocompromised settings like transplantation. Helocyte, an FBIO subsidiary, is developing this particular candidate. So far, this vaccine is in Phase 2 for CMV control in liver transplant recipients, in Phase 1/2 in pediatric hematopoietic stem cell transplantation (HSCT) recipients, and in Phase 2 in adults living with HIV and CMV. FBIO also has a trial that tests Triplex in combination with a bispecific CAR-T in non-Hodgkin lymphoma. As with Dotinurad, FBIO is also eligible for payments upon milestone achievements.

Early-to-Mid Stage Pipeline

Lastly, it's also worth mentioning that FBIO is working on earlier-stage assets as well, though these are undoubtedly inherently more speculative. Concretely, FBIO has programs with economic participation through equity and royalties, such as CMV-HIV CAR-T, a bispecific CAR-T, which is enrolling for a Phase 1 trial. Likewise, FBIO's Mustang Bio programs for recurrent glioblastoma and AJ201 for spinal and bulbar muscular atrophy (SBMA) are promising R&D projects.

Furthermore, FBIO also has IV tramadol for postoperative acute pain, as well as preclinical candidates like FB-606 for Duchenne muscular dystrophy. Other interesting pre-clinical drugs are AAV-SH for complement-mediated diseases and AAV-ATP7A for Menkes gene therapy. But, naturally, these assets aren't necessarily going to reach regulatory approval any time soon, as developing them will likely take several years. However, this doesn't mean that FBIO can't monetize them before reaching regulatory approval.

For example, in Q2 2025, FBIO sold Checkpoint to Sun Pharma, and received $28 million upfront with $4.8 million more tied to a Contingent Value Right (CVR) linked to milestones in the European approval. On top of that, FBIO is eligible for a 2.5% royalty on net sales. A Checkpoint product is Unloxcyt (cosibelimab) for cutaneous squamous cell carcinoma (cSCC), which has a BLA approved in December 2024. So, FBIO's business model can definitely result in lumpy revenues due to these types of opportunistic asset sales as well.

Valuation And Risk Analysis

Now, from a valuation perspective, FBIO currently trades at a $108.6 million market cap, so it remains decisively in microcap territory. This obviously makes it inherently riskier because it's a thinly traded stock with only 199.3 thousand shares in volume. At the current $3.45 PPS, that implies a shallow trading liquidity of just $687.6 thousand, meaning that even tiny buy/sell orders can significantly push the stock price.

However, according to Seeking Alpha's dashboard on FBIO, the company is actually projected to generate around $109.5 million in revenues this year (likely tied to a potential PRV sale; more on this later). That would price FBIO at a very attractive 1.0 P/S. For comparison, their sector's median P/S is around 3.6, so on this metric alone, FBIO already appears quite undervalued. Also, consider that FBIO's Q3 2025 balance sheet held only $86.2 million in cash, against $53.4 million in financial debt (i.e., notes payable) aside from other regular operating liabilities. So that implies an even cheaper EV of about $75.8 million, which would translate to a very compelling EV/S of 0.7. Again, this is significantly lower than its sector's median EV/S of 3.7.

Nevertheless, FBIO is not without its drawbacks at this stage. I also calculate that FBIO's Q3 2025 burned through only $6.1 million. Note that I got this figure by adding its quarterly cash flows from operations and CAPEX. But, on a TTM basis, FBIO's cash burn is actually markedly higher at approximately $66.1 million (using the same approach). And, under that more stringent cash burn estimate, FBIO's cash runway is only around 1.3 years. This is a manageable runway, but it does show that FBIO's business model isn't self-sustainable yet, which is why I can't quite rate it any higher than a speculative "Buy" at best for now.

But, ultimately, what nudges me towards a speculative "Buy" is that they have a concrete bullish catalyst in the near term. In particular, FBIO recently received a Priority Review Voucher (PRV) tied to my previously mentioned Cyprium's asset transfer to Sentynl. And remember, FBIO owns around 73.9% of Cyprium's equity. This means FBIO gained a potentially very valuable asset with that PRV, because PRVs have historically been sold between $21.2 million and $350.0 million.

Of course, that's a big price range, but compared to FBIO's market cap of just $108.6 million, even a relatively cheap price tag for its PRV would be material for investors. And, also remember that from 2020 to 2024, the average PRV traded at around $100.0 million. So, if FBIO monetizes its PRV at a similar price tag, it could easily be a transformative upside catalyst for the stock.

Conclusion: Worth Speculating

Overall, FBIO's business model is unique in its sector. They're mostly a holding company of sorts in biotech, despite the fact that they also conduct in-house R&D. But more recently, in January 2026, Cyprium's asset transfer to Sentynl created a major upside catalyst for FBIO in the near term. After all, they can now theoretically find a buyer for their newly acquired PRV and quickly monetize that into a sizeable payday for investors. Of course, this is a speculative thesis for now because we don't have any concrete disclosures or timetables regarding when they'll sell their PRV. But, reasonably speaking, I would imagine selling their PRV is high on management's priorities now, as it could quickly unlock a lot of shareholder value. But there's always the risk that the PRV sale drags on and fails to materialize, and in the meantime, FBIO continues burning through its cash reserves. That's why you should only think of FBIO as a small speculative biotech play for the foreseeable future.

data.title: Wendy's: The Market Hates The Reset - I'm Buying It
data.description:
Introduction

The last time I covered The Wendy's Company (WEN), I highlighted their revitalization efforts amid significant macro pressure, with hundreds of underperforming store closures in an effort to revitalize their brand while focusing on quality, marketing, and their international expansion.

Wendy's latest report continued this turnaround in the same direction, which the market clearly did not like, as the stock is down more than 20% since publishing the previous article, but I believe this warrants an upgrade to Strong Buy now, as Wendy's turnaround potential and value are now even more compelling, with a very strong and sustainable dividend on top.

Internal Developments

WEN's Q4 report was better than the market expected, beating on both top- and bottom-line estimates, although they still reported significant drops in the US (10.5% systemwide sales decline) despite a 6.2% international systemwide sales increase - opening 59 new international restaurants - while macro pressure is expected to continue.

As for the free cash flow, we can see them delivering $205.4 million in 2025, a 13.6% decrease compared to $237.7 million in 2024, which is still solid for a ~$1.29 billion market cap at the moment of writing this, placing them at a very attractive P/FCF ratio of ~6.3.

As for their 2026 guidance, Wendy's expects relatively flat global systemwide sales growth, with an Adjusted EBITDA of $460 million to $480 million and a total CAPEX of $120 million to $130 million (vs. ~$140 million in 2025), with a free cash flow dropping to $190 million to $205 million.

Meanwhile, their Adjusted EPS is seen dropping to $0.56 to $0.60 in 2026, well below the market's expectations, which, combined with their downsizing plan to focus on value enhancements mentioned below, led to significant target price cuts and drops in their stock price.

This is part of a wider operational refresh plan called Project Fresh, which also includes between 5% and 6% US restaurant closures in H1 2026, redirecting capital towards AUV initiatives, better marketing, a new menu framework, and a focus on their value/quality differentiators while also continuing their international expansion.

They also plan to keep paying a dividend, reiterating their commitment to it and recently declaring a $0.14 quarterly dividend (in line with previous), sustaining a roughly 8.25% dividend yield, which currently costs about $106.36 million, which is very sustainable even if their FCF drops, leading to a ~53.85% payout ratio based on their 2026 guidance's midpoint.

To be fair, I'd rather see a new CEO (they currently have an interim one) cut the dividend and dedicate a majority of these funds to their "rebuilding year," potentially even building a quality logistics system, regaining consumer trust, and even buying back shares. I like the store closures as long as they focus on the below-optimal ones (which seems to be the case), and this could be a strong step towards a potential recovery, which the company's CFO and Interim CEO also mentioned during their Q4 earnings call:

Turning to system optimization, which is about having the right footprint in each market to improve franchisee economics and enhance the customer experience. By closing consistently underperforming restaurants, we are enabling our franchisee partners to increase focus on locations with the greatest potential for profitable growth. Since we announced this program in November, we have been working with our franchisees to evaluate restaurants on a store-by-store basis and make collaborative decisions to optimize performance across the U.S. system as One Wendy's.

Financially, based on WEN's latest report, we can see an overall normal position for the industry, with the current assets now covering their current liabilities once again, and even though the debt is high, it's spread very well over the next more than half a decade, with no significant maturity that can't be refinanced, which should also buy them a good amount of time to wait for better conditions before refinancing.

Macro Developments

Especially in this case, I believe it's important to reiterate that we're not talking (only) about company-specific issues, but rather trends affecting not only the fast-food industry, but virtually everything that relies on consumers.

This weak consumer environment is normal in a high-rate market, as the role of rate hikes is to slow down the economy by lowering discretionary budgets, and fast-food is one of the first things I'd consider when tightening the belt.

As we can see based on very recent data, US consumer confidence collapsed to the lowest level since 2014, while about 32% of Americans have reportedly traded down even the most basic necessities such as food, which likely has an even bigger impact on the QSR industry since it's one of the most discretionary branches of it, which is very much visible for Wendy's - alongside concerns surrounding their quality.

In WEN's defense, they are addressing this environment as best as they can, with both better value propositions (see the new value deals above) and a focus on closing underperforming locations to focus on improving their brand image.

However, this normal weakness has been accentuated by a variety of factors affecting the dollar and US treasuries, which can in turn impact Wendy's and its consumers, including tariffs, the weakening dollar, crisis worries, Japan's economic plans that can threaten the dollar and treasuries even more - just to name a few of the headwinds coming in recent weeks - all of which can lead to even more pressure for longer, despite recent economic data boosting the market's confidence.

Another point I'd like to address is the GLP-1 threat, with recent data highlighting that households where at least one person took the medicine saw a 5.5% decrease in grocery spending six months after it, with the biggest drops seen in the calorie-dense products such as sweets and snacks, with the report highlighting that "primary dietary change appears to be a reduction in high-calorie items."

Risks Reiterated

Although I don't see GLP-1 by itself as such a big threat given the affordability and demand (5.5% drop in grocery spending if 100% of households took it is not much, and it's impossible to see this kind of adoption), I also believe that this can be just one step towards broader fundamental changes, with potential effects MAHA initiatives, GLP-1, and overall more health-conscious consumer trends, potentially leading to a mix of lower volumes and higher costs, as WEN would have to adapt to potential long-term changes.

Meanwhile, WEN has to fight in a very competitive environment, with several big names and many other contenders competing for the same limited consumer budgets, while their ownership structure can also be an issue (which has been the case in the past), as mentioned in the previous analysis, while on the day after writing this, we saw Nelson Peltz disclosing in an SEC filing that he is considering what to do with his 16% stake in WEN.

Valuation

For WEN's DCF to Equity valuation, I'll start with their $205.4 million FCF in 2025 and assume a midpoint of $197.5 million in 2026 based on their guidance (note that I'm not accounting for the net borrowings since I want to highlight their true cash flow generating potential), followed by a CAGR of 7% this time to account for more hurdles but still solid potential to recover and expand internationally, especially if the consumer environment also improves (well below the market's EPS estimates), followed by a CAGR of 5% for the subsequent 5 years, a 2.5% terminal growth rate and a very high 15% discount rate to account for competitive pressure and broader macro risks, with 190.54 million shares outstanding as of their Q4 report.

This way, we get to an equity value of $1.91 billion, for an intrinsic value of $10.03 per share, which is well above the current $6.75 at the moment of writing this despite the very high discount rate. Even if we assume significantly lower growth or even negative rates, we're still not far from the current level, which can at the very least suggest their value even as a cigar butt type of investment, while more optimistic scenarios can easily get us to the levels from a couple of years ago.

Conclusion

I'm upgrading WEN to a Strong Buy now, with the stock trading at a deep discount while the company continues to produce very strong cash flows and can even multiply if they deliver a recovery.

Again, there are risks to keep in mind, and this turnaround can obviously fail, but even as a cigar butt investment, there is still plenty of value here. I placed an order to open a position to take advantage of it and I'm looking forward to even better prices while the company works on its turnaround and the environment advances in a better direction.

data.title: NVDL: Trading On Share Price Volatility Going Into The Next Bull Wave
data.description:
GraniteShares 2x Long NVDA Daily ETF (NVDL) is a leveraged exchange-traded fund designed to provide 2x the daily performance of Nvidia (NVDA) shares. With Nvidia reporting Q4’26 earnings on February 25, 2026, I believe there may be opportunity to earn into the close of the fiscal year by strategically utilizing NVDL. Given the substantial risks involved in trading leveraged strategies, NVDL should only be used by experienced traders and for daily exposure; holding leveraged ETFs for longer than a single trading day may expose investors to compounded risk, NAV erosion, and unsalvageable losses if a trader were to go south. As a disclosure, I recently reiterated my Strong Buy rating for NVDA shares.

You can review my Q4’26 earnings preview for Nvidia here:

“Nvidia: Closing Out FY26 With A Robust Growth Opportunity (Earnings Preview)”

Setting the Stage

NVDA shares have been relatively range-bound since August 2025; I believe price action has largely been tied to uncertainty as to whether an AI bubble will burst and if Nvidia will maintain its leadership as the go-to AI accelerator stack.

I believe the AI bubble is underpinned by a number of different factors, including:

  1. High capital investment outlay for data center builds.
  2. Accelerated depreciation & amortization expense.
  3. Uncertainty as to whether enterprise AI will be adopted.
  4. Profitability, or lack thereof, by the AI developers.
  5. Novelty of AI.
  6. Circular investment cycle between the cloud services providers, semiconductor developers, and AI developers.

Starting at the top of the list, the first two factors are set to benefit Nvidia and Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) over time given the continued investment cycle in building out compute capacity tied with the replacement of older GPUs & CPUs. I believe this can lead to compounded growth in which the serviceable market is expanding and will inevitably need to be replaced. This means that Nvidia’s growth doesn’t necessarily end when the final data center is constructed, as older server racks will eventually be replaced with newer, faster, and more energy-efficient infrastructure.

The 3rd factor on the list, enterprise adoption, has been a slow-burn growth factor. I believe the market overestimated the rate of AI development to adoption, particularly given that agentic AI is designed to augment or replace human capital. With the majority of the enterprise market in the pilot phase for AI adoption, I believe there will inevitably be a ramp-up, just not as quickly as the market may be anticipating. I believe this coincides with Apple’s (AAPL) slow approach to adopting AI across its mobile devices, in which enterprises cannot risk AI applications disrupting operations if they do not function as intended. For a large enterprise, it can take multiple years to roll out an ERP system, and these systems aren’t necessarily a novel platform by comparison. I believe what’s typically overlooked is the complexity of internal processes that may result in slower agentic AI adoption, which I expect to reflect the timeframe for an ERP rollout.

The final point is the profitability of AI developers. The two major independent model developers, OpenAI (OPENAI) and Anthropic (ANTHRO), remain in the pre-profitability stage as they continue to develop more advanced model capabilities while scaling their user bases. Accordingly, Anthropic is expected to break even by 2028, while OpenAI’s path to profitability is anticipated to occur in the 2030 timeframe. I don’t believe this is necessarily a roadblock, given that these companies are positioned for high-growth/high-adoption rates rather than targeting profitability. One must bear in mind that AI is a capital-intensive business that requires a significant amount of foundational spending before realizing revenue, similar to the oil & gas shale boom in the 2010s; oil producers were capital-intensive with steep decline curves and didn’t necessarily focus on cash flow until much later in the growth cycle. I believe the AI development market is reflective of the US oil fracking market, where a substantial amount of capital is required to ramp up production before realizing a net profit.

All this relates to Nvidia; each of these factors is supportive of Nvidia’s growth trajectory, as the common theme is that more GPU & CPU capacity will be needed for continued AI development and deployment.

One theme that may be more specific to Nvidia is whether the semiconductor developer will maintain its leadership as more custom silicon designs hit the market. For example, Alphabet (GOOG) and Amazon (AMZN) each have multi-billion-dollar agreements with Anthropic for custom silicon chips and data center capacity. Though I don’t believe these deals will necessarily displace Nvidia, I can see the concerns they may raise for investors looking for a reason to turn bearish on Nvidia.

My last point on the case for Nvidia is the increasing capital outlay for compute capacity amongst the hyperscalers. I’m forecasting a 76% year-over-year growth rate for capital deployed during 2026, summing to $666 billion in spend, which is expected to largely be tied to CPUs & GPUs. I believe this should be taken as a positive indicator for the future of Nvidia’s growth trajectory given the expansive investment outlays for compute capacity. Given that GPUs have an accelerated depreciation cycle, I’m also expecting a certain degree of GPU replacement to occur going forward.

Strategically, investors may trade NVDL through the market uncertainty, particularly given NVDA shares’ range-bound trading pattern. Given the market’s directional uncertainty, traders can position through the volatility cycles, seeking price squeezes as investor sentiment turns from negative to positive. Given my positive outlook for NVDA shares, I believe NVDL can be an appealing trading strategy, as the underlying shares may present upward momentum as more clarity is brought to the market in the upcoming Q4’26 earnings release.

About GraniteShares 2x Long NVDA Daily ETF

NVDL was launched by GraniteShares on December 13, 2022 on the NASDAQ Exchange. NVDL has a net expense ratio of 105 bps and is aligned with peer leveraged strategies.

NVDL exhibits substantial liquidity with $3.8b in net assets and an average of $932mm in share value changing hands on a daily basis, making it the most liquid leveraged NVDA ETF on the market.

Risks Related to NVDL

NVDL employs swap derivatives in order to gain 200% exposure to NVDA shares, creating certain intricacies that investors must consider when trading this ETF. For example, if NVDA shares appreciate by 5% in a single day, NVDL will appreciate by 10%; the same is true to the downside, in which if NVDA shares decline by -5% in a single trading day, NVDL shares will decline by -10%. Swaps are derivative contracts that provide exposure to the performance of the underlying asset in exchange for a fixed rate. Swaps are settled on a daily basis, with performance reset on the following trading day. This reset feature is reflected in the ETF, which may result in a compounding effect when held for longer than a single trading day. The compounding effect can significantly increase risk exposure, particularly to the downside if NVDA shares were to decline over consecutive trading days. Leveraged strategies also experience a certain degree of NAV erosion as a result of the daily rebalance that may most prominently impact long-term investors, making these strategies most appropriate for daily exposure.

For example, if the underlying index were to appreciate by 2% in a single day, a 2x strategy would appreciate by 4%, meaning that an investment of $100 would increase to $104 the same trading day. If the following day results in a -2% decline in the underlying index, or a -4% decline in the leveraged strategy, the investment will decline to $99.84 the same trading day.

Traders may experience higher short-term capital gains taxes on top of exchange fees and the expense ratio, potentially impacting the total returns to the trader.

You can review additional risks here and the SEC bulletin here.

Final Thoughts

NVDL can be an appealing trading strategy for those seeking to benefit from the positive outlook for NVDA shares while incrementally trading on short-term volatility. Given the outlook for NVDA, I am recommending NVDL with a Buy rating for enhancing daily exposure. Investors could potentially utilize NVDL for longer holding periods, though they should consider the involved risks prior to considering this option.
